Conclusion: Decoupling accuracy and efficiency optimization through conditional advantage estimation effectively solves the tool overuse problem in multimodal agents, enabling both high performance and computational efficiency without the optimization conflicts of scalarized reward approaches.

Abstract: The advent of agentic multimodal models has empowered systems to actively interact with external environments. However, current agents suffer from a profound meta-cognitive deficit: they struggle to arbitrate between leveraging internal knowledge and querying external utilities. Consequently, they frequently fall prey to blind tool invocation, resorting to reflexive tool execution even when queries are resolvable from the raw visual context. This pathological behavior precipitates severe latency bottlenecks and injects extraneous noise that derails sound reasoning. Existing reinforcement learning protocols attempt to mitigate this via a scalarized reward that penalizes tool usage. Yet, this coupled formulation creates an irreconcilable optimization dilemma: an aggressive penalty suppresses essential tool use, whereas a mild penalty is entirely subsumed by the variance of the accuracy reward during advantage normalization, rendering it impotent against tool overuse. To transcend this bottleneck, we propose HDPO, a framework that reframes tool efficiency from a competing scalar objective to a strictly conditional one. By eschewing reward scalarization, HDPO maintains two orthogonal optimization channels: an accuracy channel that maximizes task correctness, and an efficiency channel that enforces execution economy exclusively within accurate trajectories via conditional advantage estimation. This decoupled architecture naturally induces a cognitive curriculum-compelling the agent to first master task resolution before refining its self-reliance. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our resulting model, Metis, reduces tool invocations by orders of magnitude while simultaneously elevating reasoning accuracy.

</details>


### [133] [When Numbers Speak: Aligning Textual Numerals and Visual Instances in Text-to-Video Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08546)
*Zhengyang Sun,Yu Chen,Xin Zhou,Xiaofan Li,Xiwu Chen,Dingkang Liang,Xiang Bai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NUMINA is a training-free framework that improves numerical alignment in text-to-video diffusion models by identifying prompt-layout inconsistencies and guiding regeneration through attention modulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-video diffusion models often struggle with generating the correct number of objects specified in prompts, creating a need for better numerical alignment without requiring retraining.

Method: NUMINA uses an identify-then-guide approach: 1) identifies prompt-layout inconsistencies by selecting discriminative self- and cross-attention heads to derive a countable latent layout, 2) refines this layout conservatively, and 3) modulates cross-attention to guide regeneration.

Result: On CountBench, NUMINA improves counting accuracy by up to 7.4% on Wan2.1-1.3B, and by 4.9% and 5.5% on 5B and 14B models respectively. It also improves CLIP alignment while maintaining temporal consistency.

Conclusion: NUMINA demonstrates that structural guidance complements existing approaches like seed search and prompt enhancement, offering a practical path toward count-accurate text-to-video diffusion without requiring model retraining.

Abstract: Text-to-video diffusion models have enabled open-ended video synthesis, but often struggle with generating the correct number of objects specified in a prompt. We introduce NUMINA , a training-free identify-then-guide framework for improved numerical alignment. NUMINA identifies prompt-layout inconsistencies by selecting discriminative self- and cross-attention heads to derive a countable latent layout. It then refines this layout conservatively and modulates cross-attention to guide regeneration. On the introduced CountBench, NUMINA improves counting accuracy by up to 7.4% on Wan2.1-1.3B, and by 4.9% and 5.5% on 5B and 14B models, respectively. Furthermore, CLIP alignment is improved while maintaining temporal consistency. These results demonstrate that structural guidance complements seed search and prompt enhancement, offering a practical path toward count-accurate text-to-video diffusion. The code is available at https://github.com/H-EmbodVis/NUMINA.

</details>


### [134] [GaussiAnimate: Reconstruct and Rig Animatable Categories with Level of Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08547)
*Jiaxin Wang,Dongxin Lyu,Zeyu Cai,Zhiyang Dou,Cheng Lin,Anpei Chen,Yuliang Xiu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Skelebones: A scaffold-skin rigging system that compresses 4D shape dynamics into controllable free-form bones with kinematic structure for character animation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Free-form bones capture non-rigid deformations well but lack intuitive kinematic control structure needed for animation. Need a system that combines deformation accuracy with controllable skeleton structure.

Method: Three-step approach: (1) Compress deformable Gaussians into free-form bones, (2) Extract Mean Curvature Skeleton from canonical Gaussians and refine it temporally, (3) Bind skeleton and bones via Partwise Motion Matching (PartMM) for novel motion synthesis.

Result: Achieves 17.3% PSNR gain over Linear Blend Skinning and 21.7% over Bag-of-Bones, with excellent reconstruction fidelity for complex non-rigid dynamics. PartMM shows 48.4% RMSE improvement over robust LBS and outperforms learning methods by >20% in low-data regimes.

Conclusion: Skelebones successfully compresses 4D shape dynamics into compact, controllable representations that balance expressiveness with intuitive control, demonstrating strong performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Abstract: Free-form bones, that conform closely to the surface, can effectively capture non-rigid deformations, but lack a kinematic structure necessary for intuitive control. Thus, we propose a Scaffold-Skin Rigging System, termed "Skelebones", with three key steps: (1) Bones: compress temporally-consistent deformable Gaussians into free-form bones, approximating non-rigid surface deformations; (2) Skeleton: extract a Mean Curvature Skeleton from canonical Gaussians and refine it temporally, ensuring a category-agnostic, motion-adaptive, and topology-correct kinematic structure; (3) Binding: bind the skeleton and bones via non-parametric partwise motion matching (PartMM), synthesizing novel bone motions by matching, retrieving, and blending existing ones. Collectively, these three steps enable us to compress the Level of Dynamics of 4D shapes into compact skelebones that are both controllable and expressive. We validate our approach on both synthetic and real-world datasets, achieving significant improvements in reanimation performance across unseen poses-with 17.3% PSNR gains over Linear Blend Skinning (LBS) and 21.7% over Bag-of-Bones (BoB)-while maintaining excellent reconstruction fidelity, particularly for characters exhibiting complex non-rigid surface dynamics. Our Partwise Motion Matching algorithm demonstrates strong generalization to both Gaussian and mesh representations, especially under low-data regime (~1000 frames), achieving 48.4% RMSE improvement over robust LBS and outperforming GRU- and MLP-based learning methods by >20%. Code will be made publicly available for research purposes at cookmaker.cn/gaussianimate.

</details>


### [135] [ETCH-X: Robustify Expressive Body Fitting to Clothed Humans with Composable Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08548)
*Xiaoben Li,Jingyi Wu,Zeyu Cai,Yu Siyuan,Boqian Li,Yuliang Xiu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ETCH-X upgrades body fitting with tightness-aware paradigm, SMPL-X extension, and dense correspondences for robust, expressive alignment of parametric models to 3D point clouds across diverse clothing, poses, and partial inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing body fitting methods lack an all-in-one solution that is both locally expressive (capturing fine details) and globally robust to real-world challenges like clothing dynamics, pose variations, and noisy/partial inputs.

Method: Upgrades ETCH with tightness-aware fitting to filter clothing dynamics, extends expressiveness with SMPL-X, replaces explicit sparse markers with implicit dense correspondences, and uses disentangled "undress" and "dense fit" modular stages trained on composable data sources.

Result: Achieves substantial improvements over ETCH on both seen data (4D-Dress: 33.0% MPJPE-All, CAPE: 35.8% V2V-Hands) and unseen data (BEDLAM2.0: 80.8% MPJPE-All, 80.5% V2V-All), with robust fitting across diverse clothing, poses, and input completeness.

Conclusion: ETCH-X provides an effective all-in-one solution for human body fitting that is both locally expressive and globally robust, enabling better downstream applications like animation and texturing.

Abstract: Human body fitting, which aligns parametric body models such as SMPL to raw 3D point clouds of clothed humans, serves as a crucial first step for downstream tasks like animation and texturing. An effective fitting method should be both locally expressive-capturing fine details such as hands and facial features-and globally robust to handle real-world challenges, including clothing dynamics, pose variations, and noisy or partial inputs. Existing approaches typically excel in only one aspect, lacking an all-in-one solution.We upgrade ETCH to ETCH-X, which leverages a tightness-aware fitting paradigm to filter out clothing dynamics ("undress"), extends expressiveness with SMPL-X, and replaces explicit sparse markers (which are highly sensitive to partial data) with implicit dense correspondences ("dense fit") for more robust and fine-grained body fitting. Our disentangled "undress" and "dense fit" modular stages enable separate and scalable training on composable data sources, including diverse simulated garments (CLOTH3D), large-scale full-body motions (AMASS), and fine-grained hand gestures (InterHand2.6M), improving outfit generalization and pose robustness of both bodies and hands. Our approach achieves robust and expressive fitting across diverse clothing, poses, and levels of input completeness, delivering a substantial performance improvement over ETCH on both: 1) seen data, such as 4D-Dress (MPJPE-All, 33.0% ) and CAPE (V2V-Hands, 35.8% ), and 2) unseen data, such as BEDLAM2.0 (MPJPE-All, 80.8% ; V2V-All, 80.5% ). Code and models will be released at https://xiaobenli00.github.io/ETCH-X/.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [136] [Evaluation as Evolution: Transforming Adversarial Diffusion into Closed-Loop Curricula for Autonomous Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07378)
*Yicheng Guo,Jiaqi Liu,Chengkai Xu,Peng Hang,Jian Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: E² is a closed-loop evolutionary framework that transforms adversarial scenario generation into an adaptive curriculum for autonomous vehicle evaluation, improving failure discovery by 9-21% while maintaining realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous vehicles suffer from safety-critical tail event scarcity in static datasets, biasing policies toward average-case behaviors and reducing robustness. Existing adversarial testing methods are open-loop and post-hoc, making it hard to incorporate discovered failures back into training.

Method: E² formulates adversarial scenario synthesis as transport-regularized sparse control over a learned reverse-time SDE prior. It uses topology-driven support selection to identify critical interacting agents and introduces Topological Anchoring to stabilize the high-dimensional generation process.

Result: E² improves collision failure discovery by 9.01% on nuScenes and up to 21.43% on nuPlan over strongest baselines, while maintaining low invalidity and high realism. It yields substantial robustness gains when boundary cases are recycled for closed-loop policy fine-tuning.

Conclusion: E² successfully transforms adversarial generation from static validation into adaptive evolutionary curriculum, enabling targeted discovery of failure cases while constraining deviations from realistic data distributions, leading to improved robustness through closed-loop fine-tuning.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles in interactive traffic environments are often limited by the scarcity of safety-critical tail events in static datasets, which biases learned policies toward average-case behaviors and reduces robustness. Existing evaluation methods attempt to address this through adversarial stress testing, but are predominantly open-loop and post-hoc, making it difficult to incorporate discovered failures back into the training process. We introduce Evaluation as Evolution ($E^2$), a closed-loop framework that transforms adversarial generation from a static validation step into an adaptive evolutionary curriculum. Specifically, $E^2$ formulates adversarial scenario synthesis as transport-regularized sparse control over a learned reverse-time SDE prior. To make this high-dimensional generation tractable, we utilize topology-driven support selection to identify critical interacting agents, and introduce Topological Anchoring to stabilize the process. This approach enables the targeted discovery of failure cases while strictly constraining deviations from realistic data distributions. Empirically, $E^2$ improves collision failure discovery by 9.01% on the nuScenes dataset and up to 21.43% on the nuPlan dataset over the strongest baselines, while maintaining low invalidity and high realism. It further yields substantial robustness gains when the resulting boundary cases are recycled for closed-loop policy fine-tuning.

</details>


### [137] [A Physical Agentic Loop for Language-Guided Grasping with Execution-State Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07395)
*Wenze Wang,Mehdi Hosseinzadeh,Feras Dayoub*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a physical agentic loop for robotic grasping that wraps learned manipulation primitives with execution monitoring and bounded recovery policies to handle failures, making language-guided grasping more robust and interpretable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robotic manipulation systems execute grasp primitives in a single-shot manner without structured feedback on failures (empty grasps, slips, wrong grasps). There's a need for embodied agents that can monitor execution states and recover from failures like digital tool-using agents do.

Method: Introduces a physical agentic loop that wraps an unmodified learned grasp-and-lift primitive with: (1) an event-based interface, (2) Watchdog execution monitoring layer that converts gripper telemetry into discrete outcome labels using contact-aware fusion and temporal stabilization, and (3) a deterministic bounded policy that finalizes, retries, or escalates to user based on outcome events and optional semantic verification.

Result: Validated on a mobile manipulator with eye-in-hand camera, keeping the underlying grasp model unchanged. The system handles visual ambiguity, distractors, and induced execution failures. Results show explicit execution-state monitoring and bounded recovery enable more robust and interpretable behavior than open-loop execution with minimal architectural overhead.

Conclusion: The physical agentic loop approach makes language-guided grasping more robust by providing structured feedback on execution failures and enabling bounded recovery policies, bridging the gap between digital agentic loops and physical robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation systems that follow language instructions often execute grasp primitives in a largely single-shot manner: a model proposes an action, the robot executes it, and failures such as empty grasps, slips, stalls, timeouts, or semantically wrong grasps are not surfaced to the decision layer in a structured way. Inspired by agentic loops in digital tool-using agents, we reformulate language-guided grasping as a bounded embodied agent operating over grounded execution states, where physical actions expose an explicit tool-state stream. We introduce a physical agentic loop that wraps an unmodified learned manipulation primitive (grasp-and-lift) with (i) an event-based interface and (ii) an execution monitoring layer, Watchdog, which converts noisy gripper telemetry into discrete outcome labels using contact-aware fusion and temporal stabilization. These outcome events, optionally combined with post-grasp semantic verification, are consumed by a deterministic bounded policy that finalizes, retries, or escalates to the user for clarification, guaranteeing finite termination. We validate the resulting loop on a mobile manipulator with an eye-in-hand D405 camera, keeping the underlying grasp model unchanged and evaluating representative scenarios involving visual ambiguity, distractors, and induced execution failures. Results show that explicit execution-state monitoring and bounded recovery enable more robust and interpretable behavior than open-loop execution, while adding minimal architectural overhead. For the source code and demo refer to our project page: https://wenzewwz123.github.io/Agentic-Loop/

</details>


### [138] [OpenPRC: A Unified Open-Source Framework for Physics-to-Task Evaluation in Physical Reservoir Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07423)
*Yogesh Phalak,Wen Sin Lor,Apoorva Khairnar,Benjamin Jantzen,Noel Naughton,Suyi Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OpenPRC is an open-source Python framework that provides a unified pipeline for Physical Reservoir Computing, enabling standardized simulation, experimental data ingestion, analysis, and optimization across diverse physical substrates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current PRC development tools are fragmented, addressing only isolated parts of the pipeline. There's a need for a unified framework that can handle both simulated and experimental data through the same interface for reproducible evaluation and physics-aware optimization across different substrates.

Method: OpenPRC uses a schema-driven physics-to-task pipeline with five modules: GPU-accelerated hybrid RK4-PBD physics engine (demlat), video-based experimental ingestion (openprc.vision), modular learning layer (reservoir), information-theoretic analysis tools (analysis), and physics-aware optimization (optimize). A universal HDF5 schema ensures reproducibility and interoperability.

Result: The framework demonstrates capabilities including simulations of Origami tessellations, video-based trajectory extraction from physical reservoirs, and provides a common interface for standardized PRC benchmarking, correlation diagnostics, and capacity analysis.

Conclusion: OpenPRC fills the gap in PRC development by providing a unified framework that serves as a standardizing layer for the community, compatible with external physics engines and enabling reproducible workflows across simulated and experimental data.

Abstract: Physical Reservoir Computing (PRC) leverages the intrinsic nonlinear dynamics of physical substrates, mechanical, optical, spintronic, and beyond, as fixed computational reservoirs, offering a compelling paradigm for energy-efficient and embodied machine learning. However, the practical workflow for developing and evaluating PRC systems remains fragmented: existing tools typically address only isolated parts of the pipeline, such as substrate-specific simulation, digital reservoir benchmarking, or readout training. What is missing is a unified framework that can represent both high-fidelity simulated trajectories and real experimental measurements through the same data interface, enabling reproducible evaluation, analysis, and physics-aware optimization across substrates and data sources. We present OpenPRC, an open-source Python framework that fills this gap through a schema-driven physics-to-task pipeline built around five modules: a GPU-accelerated hybrid RK4-PBD physics engine (demlat), a video-based experimental ingestion layer (openprc.vision), a modular learning layer (reservoir), information-theoretic analysis and benchmarking tools (analysis), and physics-aware optimization (optimize). A universal HDF5 schema enforces reproducibility and interoperability, allowing GPU-simulated and experimentally acquired trajectories to enter the same downstream workflow without modification. Demonstrated capabilities include simulations of Origami tessellations, video-based trajectory extraction from a physical reservoir, and a common interface for standardized PRC benchmarking, correlation diagnostics, and capacity analysis. The longer-term vision is to serve as a standardizing layer for the PRC community, compatible with external physics engines including PyBullet, PyElastica, and MERLIN.

</details>


### [139] [CMP: Robust Whole-Body Tracking for Loco-Manipulation via Competence Manifold Projection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07457)
*Ziyang Cheng,Haoyu Wei,Hang Yin,Xiuwei Xu,Bingyao Yu,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CMP (Competence Manifold Projection) improves robustness of whole-body control policies for legged manipulators against OOD inputs while maintaining task performance, using efficient safety constraints and isomorphic latent space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning-based whole-body control policies for legged mobile manipulators are fragile against Out-of-Distribution (OOD) inputs from sensor noise or infeasible user commands, leading to catastrophic failures.

Method: Proposes Competence Manifold Projection (CMP) with: 1) Frame-Wise Safety Scheme transforming infinite-horizon safety constraints into single-step manifold inclusion; 2) Lower-Bounded Safety Estimator distinguishing unmastered intentions; 3) Isomorphic Latent Space aligning manifold geometry with safety probability for O(1) defense.

Result: CMP achieves up to 10-fold survival rate improvement in OOD scenarios where baselines fail catastrophically, with under 10% tracking degradation. System shows emergent "best-effort" generalization behaviors to progressively accomplish OOD goals.

Conclusion: CMP provides an effective framework for robust whole-body control of legged manipulators against OOD perturbations, balancing safety and performance while enabling graceful degradation and best-effort goal achievement.

Abstract: While decoupled control schemes for legged mobile manipulators have shown robustness, learning holistic whole-body control policies for tracking global end-effector poses remains fragile against Out-of-Distribution (OOD) inputs induced by sensor noise or infeasible user commands. To improve robustness against these perturbations without sacrificing task performance and continuity, we propose Competence Manifold Projection (CMP). Specifically, we utilize a Frame-Wise Safety Scheme that transforms the infinite-horizon safety constraint into a computationally efficient single-step manifold inclusion. To instantiate this competence manifold, we employ a Lower-Bounded Safety Estimator that distinguishes unmastered intentions from the training distribution. We then introduce an Isomorphic Latent Space (ILS) that aligns manifold geometry with safety probability, enabling efficient O(1) seamless defense against arbitrary OOD intents. Experiments demonstrate that CMP achieves up to a 10-fold survival rate improvement in typical OOD scenarios where baselines suffer catastrophic failure, incurring under 10% tracking degradation. Notably, the system exhibits emergent ``best-effort'' generalization behaviors to progressively accomplish OOD goals by adhering to the competence boundaries. Result videos are available at: https://shepherd1226.github.io/CMP.

</details>


### [140] [Active Reward Machine Inference From Raw State Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07480)
*Mohamad Louai Shehab,Antoine Aspeel,Necmiye Ozay*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Learning reward machines from raw state and policy data without access to rewards, labels, or machine nodes, with extension to active learning for efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward machines help with multi-stage tasks in reinforcement learning, but manually specifying them (including labeling functions) is difficult and time-consuming.

Method: Develop approach to learn reward machines directly from raw state and policy trajectory data without observing rewards, labels, or machine nodes. Extend to active learning by incrementally querying trajectory extensions to improve data efficiency.

Result: Show what trajectory data is sufficient for learning reward machines in information-scarce regime. Demonstrate results with grid world examples showing successful learning.

Conclusion: Reward machines can be learned from minimal trajectory data without access to traditional supervision signals, and active learning further improves data and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Reward machines are automaton-like structures that capture the memory required to accomplish a multi-stage task. When combined with reinforcement learning or optimal control methods, they can be used to synthesize robot policies to achieve such tasks. However, specifying a reward machine by hand, including a labeling function capturing high-level features that the decisions are based on, can be a daunting task. This paper deals with the problem of learning reward machines directly from raw state and policy information. As opposed to existing works, we assume no access to observations of rewards, labels, or machine nodes, and show what trajectory data is sufficient for learning the reward machine in this information-scarce regime. We then extend the result to an active learning setting where we incrementally query trajectory extensions to improve data (and indirectly computational) efficiency. Results are demonstrated with several grid world examples.

</details>


### [141] [Grasp as You Dream: Imitating Functional Grasping from Generated Human Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07517)
*Chao Tang,Jiacheng Xu,Haofei Lu,Bolin Zou,Wenlong Dong,Hong Zhang,Danica Kragic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GraspDreamer enables zero-shot functional grasping by using visual generative models to synthesize human demonstrations, avoiding labor-intensive real-world data collection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Building generalist robots for functional grasping in open-world environments is challenging due to object/task diversity. Existing methods are either too narrow or require prohibitively large-scale data collection.

Method: Leverages human demonstrations synthesized by visual generative models (VGMs) pre-trained on internet-scale human data, which encode generalized priors about human-object interactions. Combines these priors with embodiment-specific action optimization for zero-shot functional grasping.

Result: Superior data efficiency and generalization performance compared to previous methods on public benchmarks with different robot hands. Real-world evaluations validate effectiveness on real robots. Can be extended to downstream manipulation tasks and generate data for visuomotor policy learning.

Conclusion: GraspDreamer provides an efficient alternative to large-scale data collection by leveraging generative models' priors about human interactions, enabling zero-shot functional grasping with strong generalization capabilities.

Abstract: Building generalist robots capable of performing functional grasping in everyday, open-world environments remains a significant challenge due to the vast diversity of objects and tasks. Existing methods are either constrained to narrow object/task sets or rely on prohibitively large-scale data collection to capture real-world variability. In this work, we present an alternative approach, GraspDreamer, a method that leverages human demonstrations synthesized by visual generative models (VGMs) (e.g., video generation models) to enable zero-shot functional grasping without labor-intensive data collection. The key idea is that VGMs pre-trained on internet-scale human data implicitly encode generalized priors about how humans interact with the physical world, which can be combined with embodiment-specific action optimization to enable functional grasping with minimal effort. Extensive experiments on the public benchmarks with different robot hands demonstrate the superior data efficiency and generalization performance of GraspDreamer compared to previous methods. Real-world evaluations further validate the effectiveness on real robots. Additionally, we showcase that GraspDreamer can (1) be naturally extended to downstream manipulation tasks, and (2) can generate data to support visuomotor policy learning.

</details>


### [142] [Robust Multi-Agent Target Tracking in Intermittent Communication Environments via Analytical Belief Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07575)
*Mohamed Abdelnaby,Samuel Honor,Kevin Leahy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents analytical solutions for decentralized belief merging in multi-agent target tracking, eliminating optimization artifacts and reducing computational complexity while introducing a spatially-aware visit-weighted KL merging strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In GPS-denied, communication-restricted environments (underwater, subterranean, adversarial domains), agents must operate independently with limited information exchange. Transmitting complete observation histories is bandwidth-exhaustive, so exchanging probabilistic belief maps is more efficient. Traditional divergence minimization approaches using numerical solvers introduce quantization errors and artificial noise floors.

Method: Formulates decentralized belief merging as Forward and Reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence optimizations and derives exact closed-form analytical solutions. Proposes a novel spatially-aware visit-weighted KL merging strategy that dynamically weighs agent beliefs based on their physical visitation history.

Result: Mathematically eliminates optimization artifacts, achieving perfect mathematical fidelity while reducing computational complexity to O(N|S|) scalar operations. Extensive simulations (tens of thousands) show the method significantly suppresses sensor noise and outperforms standard analytical means in environments with degraded sensors and prolonged communication intervals.

Conclusion: The proposed analytical solutions for decentralized belief merging provide mathematically exact, computationally efficient methods that outperform traditional approaches, particularly in challenging environments with limited communication and degraded sensors.

Abstract: Autonomous multi-agent target tracking in GPS-denied and communication-restricted environments (e.g., underwater exploration, subterranean search and rescue, and adversarial domains) forces agents to operate independently and only exchange information during brief reconnection windows. Because transmitting complete observation and trajectory histories is bandwidth-exhaustive, exchanging probabilistic belief maps serves as a highly efficient proxy that preserves the topology of agent knowledge. While minimizing divergence metrics to merge these decentralized beliefs is conceptually sound, traditional approaches often rely on numerical solvers that introduce critical quantization errors and artificial noise floors. In this paper, we formulate the decentralized belief merging problem as Forward and Reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence optimizations and derive their exact closed-form analytical solutions. By deploying these derivations, we mathematically eliminate optimization artifacts, achieving perfect mathematical fidelity while reducing the computational complexity of the belief merge to $\mathcal{O}(N|S|)$ scalar operations. Furthermore, we propose a novel spatially-aware visit-weighted KL merging strategy that dynamically weighs agent beliefs based on their physical visitation history. Validated across tens of thousands of distributed simulations, extensive sensitivity analysis demonstrates that our proposed method significantly suppresses sensor noise and outperforms standard analytical means in environments characterized by highly degraded sensors and prolonged communication intervals.

</details>


### [143] [Spatio-Temporal Grounding of Large Language Models from Perception Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07592)
*Jacob Anderson,Bardh Hoxha,Georgios Fainekos,Hideki Okamoto,Danil Prokhorov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FESTS framework injects verifiable spatio-temporal supervision into LLMs by compiling natural-language queries into Spatial Regular Expressions, enabling unlimited training data without manual labels and achieving GPT-4.1-level performance with a much smaller model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current smaller LLMs struggle with fine-grained spatial relations, metric distances, and temporal orderings needed for embodied AI agents to reason about object movement and interactions in 3D space over time.

Method: Introduces FESTS framework that compiles natural-language queries into Spatial Regular Expressions (SpRE) combining regex syntax with S4u spatial logic and quantification. The pipeline matches SpRE against structured video logs to generate aligned (query, frames, match, explanation) tuples for training.

Result: Training a 3B-parameter model on 27k FESTS tuples boosts frame-level F1 from 48.5% to 87.5%, matching GPT-4.1 on complex spatio-temporal reasoning while being two orders of magnitude smaller.

Conclusion: FESTS enables effective spatio-temporal intelligence for Video LLMs by providing verifiable supervision without manual labeling, allowing smaller models to achieve state-of-the-art performance in spatial-temporal reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Embodied-AI agents must reason about how objects move and interact in 3-D space over time, yet existing smaller frontier Large Language Models (LLMs) still mis-handle fine-grained spatial relations, metric distances, and temporal orderings. We introduce the general framework Formally Explainable Spatio-Temporal Scenes (FESTS) that injects verifiable spatio-temporal supervision into an LLM by compiling natural-language queries into Spatial Regular Expression (SpRE) -- a language combining regular expression syntax with S4u spatial logic and extended here with universal and existential quantification. The pipeline matches each SpRE against any structured video log and exports aligned (query, frames, match, explanation) tuples, enabling unlimited training data without manual labels. Training a 3-billion-parameter model on 27k such tuples boosts frame-level F1 from 48.5% to 87.5%, matching GPT-4.1 on complex spatio-temporal reasoning while remaining two orders of magnitude smaller, and, hence, enabling spatio-temporal intelligence for Video LLM.

</details>


### [144] [SANDO: Safe Autonomous Trajectory Planning for Dynamic Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07599)
*Kota Kondo,Jesús Tordesillas,Jonathan P. How*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SANDO is a safe trajectory planner for 3D dynamic unknown environments that guarantees collision-free paths while maintaining fast replanning through heat map-based global planning, spatiotemporal safe flight corridors, and efficient MIQP optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing planners face a trade-off: soft-constraint methods are fast but cannot guarantee safety, while hard-constraint methods ensure safety at the cost of slow computation. This is problematic for dynamic unknown environments where obstacles are unknown and plans can become unsafe at any moment.

Method: Three key contributions: 1) Heat map-based A* global planner with soft costs + spatiotemporal safe flight corridors (STSFC) that inflate obstacles only by worst-case reachable set per time layer, 2) MIQP trajectory optimization with hard collision constraints + variable elimination for speedup, 3) Formal safety analysis with explicit velocity and estimation error assumptions.

Result: Variable elimination yields 7.4x speedup; STSFC critical for feasibility in dense dynamic environments. SANDO achieves highest success rate with zero constraint violations across all benchmarks (static, obstacle-rich, dynamic). Hardware experiments: 6 safe flights in static + 10 safe flights among dynamic obstacles with onboard planning.

Conclusion: SANDO successfully addresses the safety-speed trade-off in 3D dynamic unknown environments by combining efficient global planning, time-layered obstacle inflation, fast optimization, and formal safety guarantees, demonstrating robust performance in simulation and real-world UAV flights.

Abstract: SANDO is a safe trajectory planner for 3D dynamic unknown environments, where obstacle locations and motions are unknown a priori and a collision-free plan can become unsafe at any moment, requiring fast replanning. Existing soft-constraint planners are fast but cannot guarantee collision-free paths, while hard-constraint methods ensure safety at the cost of longer computation. SANDO addresses this trade-off through three contributions. First, a heat map-based A* global planner steers paths away from high-risk regions using soft costs, and a spatiotemporal safe flight corridor (STSFC) generator produces time-layered polytopes that inflate obstacles only by their worst-case reachable set at each time layer, rather than by the worst case over the entire horizon. Second, trajectory optimization is formulated as a Mixed-Integer Quadratic Program (MIQP) with hard collision-avoidance constraints, and a variable elimination technique reduces the number of decision variables, enabling fast computation. Third, a formal safety analysis establishes collision-free guarantees under explicit velocity-bound and estimation-error assumptions. Ablation studies show that variable elimination yields up to 7.4x speedup in optimization time, and that STSFCs are critical for feasibility in dense dynamic environments. Benchmark simulations against state-of-the-art methods across standardized static benchmarks, obstacle-rich static forests, and dynamic environments show that SANDO consistently achieves the highest success rate with no constraint violations across all difficulty levels; perception-only experiments without ground truth obstacle information confirm robust performance under realistic sensing. Hardware experiments on a UAV with fully onboard planning, perception, and localization demonstrate six safe flights in static environments and ten safe flights among dynamic obstacles.

</details>


### [145] [EgoVerse: An Egocentric Human Dataset for Robot Learning from Around the World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07607)
*Ryan Punamiya,Simar Kareer,Zeyi Liu,Josh Citron,Ri-Zhao Qiu,Xiongyi Cai,Alexey Gavryushin,Jiaqi Chen,Davide Liconti,Lawrence Y. Zhu,Patcharapong Aphiwetsa,Baoyu Li,Aniketh Cheluva,Pranav Kuppili,Yangcen Liu,Dhruv Patel,Aidan Gao,Hye-Young Chung,Ryan Co,Renee Zbizika,Jeff Liu,Xiaomeng Xu,Haoyu Xiong,Geng Chen,Sebastiano Oliani,Chenyu Yang,Xi Wang,James Fort,Richard Newcombe,Josh Gao,Jason Chong,Garrett Matsuda,Aseem Doriwala,Marc Pollefeys,Robert Katzschmann,Xiaolong Wang,Shuran Song,Judy Hoffman,Danfei Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EgoVerse is a collaborative platform for human data-driven robot learning that unifies data collection, processing, and access, featuring 1,362 hours of human demonstrations across diverse tasks, scenes, and demonstrators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot learning requires large-scale data, but robot data collection is expensive and difficult to scale. Egocentric human data offers rich manipulation behavior, but existing datasets are limited in scope, difficult to extend, and fragmented across institutions.

Method: Introduces EgoVerse, a collaborative platform that unifies data collection, processing, and access under a shared framework, enabling contributions from individual researchers, academic labs, and industry partners. Includes standardized formats, manipulation-relevant annotations, and tooling for downstream learning.

Result: Current release includes 1,362 hours (80k episodes) of human demonstrations spanning 1,965 tasks, 240 scenes, and 2,087 unique demonstrators. Large-scale study shows policy performance generally improves with increased human data, but effective scaling depends on alignment between human data and robot learning objectives.

Conclusion: EgoVerse establishes a foundation for reproducible progress in human data-driven robot learning through its dataset, platform, and collaborative study framework, addressing scalability challenges in robot learning data collection.

Abstract: Robot learning increasingly depends on large and diverse data, yet robot data collection remains expensive and difficult to scale. Egocentric human data offer a promising alternative by capturing rich manipulation behavior across everyday environments. However, existing human datasets are often limited in scope, difficult to extend, and fragmented across institutions. We introduce EgoVerse, a collaborative platform for human data-driven robot learning that unifies data collection, processing, and access under a shared framework, enabling contributions from individual researchers, academic labs, and industry partners. The current release includes 1,362 hours (80k episodes) of human demonstrations spanning 1,965 tasks, 240 scenes, and 2,087 unique demonstrators, with standardized formats, manipulation-relevant annotations, and tooling for downstream learning. Beyond the dataset, we conduct a large-scale study of human-to-robot transfer with experiments replicated across multiple labs, tasks, and robot embodiments under shared protocols. We find that policy performance generally improves with increased human data, but that effective scaling depends on alignment between human data and robot learning objectives. Together, the dataset, platform, and study establish a foundation for reproducible progress in human data-driven robot learning. Videos and additional information can be found at https://egoverse.ai/

</details>


### [146] [Safe Large-Scale Robust Nonlinear MPC in Milliseconds via Reachability-Constrained System Level Synthesis on the GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07644)
*Jeffrey Fang,Glen Chou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GPU-SLS is a GPU-parallelized framework for real-time robust nonlinear MPC that jointly optimizes nominal trajectories, tracking controllers, and reachable sets, achieving 100% safety on high-dimensional robotic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a need for real-time robust control for high-dimensional robotic systems (like quadrupeds and humanoids) that can handle uncertainty while maintaining safety guarantees, which existing methods struggle with due to computational complexity.

Method: Develops a GPU-parallelized framework using sequential quadratic programming with a novel GPU-accelerated QP solver based on ADMM with parallel associative scans and adaptive caching. Uses the same GPU QP backend for optimizing robust tracking controllers and closed-loop reachable sets via system level synthesis (SLS).

Result: Achieves 97.7% reduction in nominal trajectory solve times vs CPU solvers, 71.8% vs GPU solvers, and 237x acceleration for SLS-based control and reachability. Maintains 100% empirical safety, handles 61D quadrupeds and 75D humanoids with 20ms average solve times, scaling to 2×10⁵ decision variables and 8×10⁴ constraints.

Conclusion: GPU-SLS enables real-time robust MPC for high-dimensional robotic systems with guaranteed safety, significantly outperforming existing methods in both speed and reliability while scaling to complex problems previously intractable for real-time applications.

Abstract: We present GPU-SLS, a GPU-parallelized framework for safe, robust nonlinear model predictive control (MPC) that scales to high-dimensional uncertain robotic systems and long planning horizons. Our method jointly optimizes an inequality-constrained, dynamically-feasible nominal trajectory, a tracking controller, and a closed-loop reachable set under disturbance, all in real-time. To efficiently compute nominal trajectories, we develop a sequential quadratic programming procedure with a novel GPU-accelerated quadratic program (QP) solver that uses parallel associative scans and adaptive caching within an alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) framework. The same GPU QP backend is used to optimize robust tracking controllers and closed-loop reachable sets via system level synthesis (SLS), enabling reachability-constrained control in both fixed- and receding-horizon settings. We achieve substantial performance gains, reducing nominal trajectory solve times by 97.7% relative to state-of-the-art CPU solvers and 71.8% compared to GPU solvers, while accelerating SLS-based control and reachability by 237x. Despite large problem scales, our method achieves 100% empirical safety, unlike high-dimensional learning-based reachability baselines. We validate our approach on complex nonlinear systems, including whole-body quadrupeds (61D) and humanoids (75D), synthesizing robust control policies online on the GPU in 20 milliseconds on average and scaling to problems with 2 x 10^5 decision variables and 8 x 10^4 constraints. The implementation of our method is available at https://github.com/Jeff300fang/gpu_sls.

</details>


### [147] [Reset-Free Reinforcement Learning for Real-World Agile Driving: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07672)
*Kohei Honda,Hirotaka Hosogaya*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Reset-free RL for real-world agile driving shows simulation-to-reality gap: TD-MPC2 outperforms MPPI baseline physically, while simulation-best SAC with residual learning fails to transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-speed driving near tire friction limits is challenging for RL due to complex dynamics, actuation delays, and unmodeled effects that hinder simulation accuracy and sim-to-real transfer. Need for autonomous training without manual resets on physical platforms.

Method: Used Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control as both reset policy and base policy for residual learning. Systematically compared three RL algorithms (PPO, SAC, TD-MPC2) with/without residual learning in simulation and real-world experiments on 1/10-scale vehicle on slippery indoor track.

Result: Clear simulation-to-reality gap: SAC with residual learning achieved highest returns in simulation, but only TD-MPC2 consistently outperformed MPPI baseline on physical platform. Residual learning beneficial in simulation but failed to transfer advantage to real world, sometimes degrading performance.

Conclusion: Reset-free RL in real world poses unique challenges absent from simulation, requiring further algorithmic development tailored to training in the wild. TD-MPC2 shows promise for real-world agile driving applications.

Abstract: This paper presents an empirical study of reset-free reinforcement learning (RL) for real-world agile driving, in which a physical 1/10-scale vehicle learns continuously on a slippery indoor track without manual resets. High-speed driving near the limits of tire friction is particularly challenging for learning-based methods because complex vehicle dynamics, actuation delays, and other unmodeled effects hinder both accurate simulation and direct sim-to-real transfer of learned policies. To enable autonomous training on a physical platform, we employ Model Predictive Path Integral control (MPPI) as both the reset policy and the base policy for residual learning, and systematically compare three representative RL algorithms, i.e., PPO, SAC, and TD-MPC2, with and without residual learning in simulation and real-world experiments. Our results reveal a clear gap between simulation and real-world: SAC with residual learning achieves the highest returns in simulation, yet only TD-MPC2 consistently outperforms the MPPI baseline on the physical platform. Moreover, residual learning, while clearly beneficial in simulation, fails to transfer its advantage to the real world and can even degrade performance. These findings reveal that reset-free RL in the real world poses unique challenges absent from simulation, calling for further algorithmic development tailored to training in the wild.

</details>


### [148] [Bird-Inspired Spatial Flapping Wing Mechanism via Coupled Linkages with Single Actuator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07677)
*Daniel Huczala,Sun-Pill Jung,Frank C. Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A single-motor flapping-wing mechanism using coupled Bennett linkages achieves coordinated sweep-and-fold wing motion through passive state switching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial single-loop mechanisms like Bennett linkages offer 1-DOF actuation with spatial trajectories ideal for lightweight bio-inspired robotics, but their geometric synthesis is mathematically complex and often avoided in engineering tasks.

Method: Developed a bird-inspired flapping-wing mechanism using two coupled spatial four-bars driven by one motor. One linkage generates spatial sweeping stroke, while the serially coupled linkage remains unactuated and passively switches between extended/folded wing configurations. Introduced simplified kinematic methodology for constructing Bennett linkages from quadrilaterals with desired surface area.

Result: Successfully created a 3D-printed prototype that demonstrates intended spatial stroke and passive folding behavior, achieving coordinated sweep-and-fold wing motion with single actuation input.

Conclusion: The architecture reduces weight and control complexity by leveraging mechanically induced passive state switching, enabling efficient bio-inspired robotic wing design with simplified Bennett linkage construction.

Abstract: Spatial single-loop mechanisms such as Bennett linkages offer a unique combination of one-degree-of-freedom actuation and nontrivial spatial trajectories, making them attractive for lightweight bio-inspired robotic design. However, although they appear simple and elegant, the geometric task-based synthesis is rather complicated and often avoided in engineering tasks due to the mathematical complexity involved. This paper presents a bird-inspired flapping-wing mechanism built from two coupled spatial four-bars, driven by a single motor. One linkage is actuated to generate the desired spatial sweeping stroke, while the serially coupled linkage remains unactuated and passively switches between extended and folded wing configurations over the stroke cycle. We introduce a simplified kinematic methodology for constructing Bennett linkages from quadrilaterals that contain a desired surface area and further leverage mechanically induced passive state switching. This architecture realizes a coordinated sweep-and-fold wing motion with a single actuation input, reducing weight and control complexity. A 3D-printed prototype is assembled and tested, demonstrating the intended spatial stroke and passive folding behavior.

</details>


### [149] [Vision-Language Navigation for Aerial Robots: Towards the Era of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07705)
*Xingyu Xia,Lekai Zhou,Yujie Tang,Xiaozhou Zhu,Hai Zhu,Wen Yao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This survey paper provides a comprehensive review of Aerial Vision-and-Language Navigation (Aerial VLN), analyzing methods, evaluation infrastructure, and identifying key open problems in the field, with particular focus on recent LLM/VLM integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aerial VLN aims to enable UAVs to interpret natural language instructions and navigate 3D environments by grounding language in visual perception. The field has evolved with LLM/VLM integration, creating a need for systematic analysis of methods, evaluation infrastructure, and identification of research gaps.

Method: The survey organizes Aerial VLN methods into a taxonomy of five architectural categories: sequence-to-sequence/attention-based, end-to-end LLM/VLM, hierarchical, multi-agent, and dialog-based navigation. It systematically analyzes each category's design rationales, technical trade-offs, and performance. The paper also critically assesses evaluation infrastructure including datasets, simulation platforms, and metrics.

Result: The survey consolidates cross-method comparisons on shared benchmarks and analyzes key architectural trade-offs (discrete vs continuous actions, end-to-end vs hierarchical designs, simulation-to-reality gap). It identifies gaps in current evaluation infrastructure regarding scale, environmental diversity, real-world grounding, and metric coverage.

Conclusion: The paper synthesizes seven concrete open problems: long-horizon instruction grounding, viewpoint robustness, scalable spatial representation, continuous 6-DoF action execution, onboard deployment, benchmark standardization, and multi-UAV swarm navigation, providing specific research directions grounded in the evidence presented throughout the survey.

Abstract: Aerial vision-and-language navigation (Aerial VLN) aims to enable unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to interpret natural language instructions and autonomously navigate complex three-dimensional environments by grounding language in visual perception. This survey provides a critical and analytical review of the Aerial VLN field, with particular attention to the recent integration of large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). We first formally introduce the Aerial VLN problem and define two interaction paradigms: single-instruction and dialog-based, as foundational axes. We then organize the body of Aerial VLN methods into a taxonomy of five architectural categories: sequence-to-sequence and attention-based methods, end-to-end LLM/VLM methods, hierarchical methods, multi-agent methods, and dialog-based navigation methods. For each category, we systematically analyze design rationales, technical trade-offs, and reported performance. We critically assess the evaluation infrastructure for Aerial VLN, including datasets, simulation platforms, and metrics, and identify their gaps in scale, environmental diversity, real-world grounding, and metric coverage. We consolidate cross-method comparisons on shared benchmarks and analyze key architectural trade-offs, including discrete versus continuous actions, end-to-end versus hierarchical designs, and the simulation-to-reality gap. Finally, we synthesize seven concrete open problems: long-horizon instruction grounding, viewpoint robustness, scalable spatial representation, continuous 6-DoF action execution, onboard deployment, benchmark standardization, and multi-UAV swarm navigation, with specific research directions grounded in the evidence presented throughout the survey.

</details>


### [150] [RoboAgent: Chaining Basic Capabilities for Embodied Task Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07774)
*Peiran Xu,Jiaqi Zheng,Yadong Mu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboAgent: A capability-driven planning pipeline for embodied task planning that decomposes complex tasks into basic vision-language problems using a single VLM with multiple sub-capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle with embodied task planning due to challenges in multi-turn interaction, long-horizon reasoning, and extended context analysis. There's a gap between VLMs' multimodal understanding capabilities and their application to complex embodied planning tasks.

Method: Proposes RoboAgent with a capability-driven planning pipeline where a scheduler invokes different sub-capabilities (each with its own context) to decompose complex planning into basic vision-language problems. Uses a single VLM for all components. Training involves three stages: (1) behavior cloning with expert plans, (2) DAgger training using model-collected trajectories, and (3) reinforcement learning guided by expert policy, with simulator information used for supervision and augmented/synthetic data for diversity.

Result: Extensive experiments on widely used embodied task planning benchmarks validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, showing improved performance on complex embodied planning tasks.

Conclusion: RoboAgent successfully bridges the gap between VLMs' multimodal understanding and embodied planning by decomposing complex tasks into manageable sub-problems, enabling more transparent and controllable reasoning while maintaining a single-model architecture without external tools.

Abstract: This paper focuses on embodied task planning, where an agent acquires visual observations from the environment and executes atomic actions to accomplish a given task. Although recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved impressive results in multimodal understanding and reasoning, their performance remains limited when applied to embodied planning that involves multi-turn interaction, long-horizon reasoning, and extended context analysis. To bridge this gap, we propose RoboAgent, a capability-driven planning pipeline in which the model actively invokes different sub-capabilities. Each capability maintains its own context, and produces intermediate reasoning results or interacts with the environment according to the query given by a scheduler. This framework decomposes complex planning into a sequence of basic vision-language problems that VLMs can better address, enabling a more transparent and controllable reasoning process. The scheduler and all capabilities are implemented with a single VLM, without relying on external tools. To train this VLM, we adopt a multi-stage paradigm that consists of: (1) behavior cloning with expert plans, (2) DAgger training using trajectories collected by the model, and (3) reinforcement learning guided by an expert policy. Across these stages, we exploit the internal information of the environment simulator to construct high-quality supervision for each capability, and we further introduce augmented and synthetic data to enhance the model's performance in more diverse scenarios. Extensive experiments on widely used embodied task planning benchmarks validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Our codes will be available at https://github.com/woyut/RoboAgent_CVPR26.

</details>


### [151] [Learning Without Losing Identity: Capability Evolution for Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07799)
*Xue Qin,Simin Luan,John See,Cong Yang,Zhijun Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A capability-centric evolution paradigm for embodied agents that separates agent identity from capability improvement through modular, versioned Embodied Capability Modules (ECMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches modify the agent itself (prompt engineering, policy updates, structural redesign), causing instability and loss of identity in long-lived systems. There's a need for persistent agent identity while enabling continuous improvement.

Method: Introduces Embodied Capability Modules (ECMs) - modular, versioned units of embodied functionality. Uses a unified framework where capability evolution is decoupled from agent identity through a closed-loop process of task execution, experience collection, model refinement, and module updating, with runtime safety enforcement.

Result: In simulated embodied tasks, capability evolution improved task success rates from 32.4% to 91.3% over 20 iterations, outperforming agent-modification baselines and established skill-learning methods (SPiRL, SkiMo), while preserving zero policy drift and zero safety violations.

Conclusion: Separating agent identity from capability evolution provides a scalable and safe foundation for long-term embodied intelligence, enabling continuous improvement without compromising system stability or identity.

Abstract: Embodied agents are expected to operate persistently in dynamic physical environments, continuously acquiring new capabilities over time. Existing approaches to improving agent performance often rely on modifying the agent itself -- through prompt engineering, policy updates, or structural redesign -- leading to instability and loss of identity in long-lived systems. In this work, we propose a capability-centric evolution paradigm for embodied agents. We argue that a robot should maintain a persistent agent as its cognitive identity, while enabling continuous improvement through the evolution of its capabilities. Specifically, we introduce the concept of Embodied Capability Modules (ECMs), which represent modular, versioned units of embodied functionality that can be learned, refined, and composed over time. We present a unified framework in which capability evolution is decoupled from agent identity. Capabilities evolve through a closed-loop process involving task execution, experience collection, model refinement, and module updating, while all executions are governed by a runtime layer that enforces safety and policy constraints. We demonstrate through simulated embodied tasks that capability evolution improves task success rates from 32.4% to 91.3% over 20 iterations, outperforming both agent-modification baselines and established skill-learning methods (SPiRL, SkiMo), while preserving zero policy drift and zero safety violations. Our results suggest that separating agent identity from capability evolution provides a scalable and safe foundation for long-term embodied intelligence.

</details>


### [152] [Harnessing Embodied Agents: Runtime Governance for Policy-Constrained Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07833)
*Xue Qin,Simin Luan,John See,Cong Yang,Zhijun Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a runtime governance framework that separates agent cognition from execution oversight to keep embodied agents' actions governable, showing 96.2% interception of unauthorized actions in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As embodied agents evolve into active executors interacting with physical environments, granting them execution authority creates a challenge: how to keep their actions governable at runtime. Existing approaches embed safety logic inside the agent loop, making execution control difficult to standardize, audit, and adapt.

Method: Proposes a policy-constrained execution framework that externalizes governance into a dedicated runtime layer. This layer performs policy checking, capability admission, execution monitoring, rollback handling, and human override. Formalizes the control boundary among the embodied agent, Embodied Capability Modules (ECMs), and runtime governance layer.

Result: Validated through 1000 randomized simulation trials across three governance dimensions: 96.2% interception of unauthorized actions, reduction of unsafe continuation from 100% to 22.2% under runtime drift, and 91.4% recovery success with full policy compliance. Substantially outperformed all baselines (p<0.001).

Conclusion: Embodied intelligence requires not only stronger agents but stronger runtime governance. By reframing runtime governance as a first-class systems problem, policy-constrained execution should be a key design principle for embodied agent systems.

Abstract: Embodied agents are evolving from passive reasoning systems into active executors that interact with tools, robots, and physical environments. Once granted execution authority, the central challenge becomes how to keep actions governable at runtime. Existing approaches embed safety and recovery logic inside the agent loop, making execution control difficult to standardize, audit, and adapt.
  This paper argues that embodied intelligence requires not only stronger agents, but stronger runtime governance. We propose a framework for policy-constrained execution that separates agent cognition from execution oversight. Governance is externalized into a dedicated runtime layer performing policy checking, capability admission, execution monitoring, rollback handling, and human override.
  We formalize the control boundary among the embodied agent, Embodied Capability Modules (ECMs), and runtime governance layer, and validate through 1000 randomized simulation trials across three governance dimensions. Results show 96.2% interception of unauthorized actions, reduction of unsafe continuation from 100% to 22.2% under runtime drift, and 91.4% recovery success with full policy compliance, substantially outperforming all baselines (p<0.001). By reframing runtime governance as a first-class systems problem, this paper positions policy-constrained execution as a key design principle for embodied agent systems.

</details>


### [153] [The Sustainability Gap in Robotics: A Large-Scale Survey of Sustainability Awareness in 50,000 Research Articles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07921)
*Antun Skuric,Leandro Von Werra,Thomas Wolf*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Large-scale survey of 50k robotics papers shows sustainability mentions remain below 2%, SDG references below 0.1%, and sustainability-motivated papers below 5%, revealing a gap between robotics' potential and stated sustainability intent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify how often robotics research papers mention social, ecological, and sustainability impacts, and analyze their alignment with UN Sustainable Development Goals, revealing the gap between the field's potential and its stated sustainability intent.

Method: Analyzed nearly 50,000 open-access papers from arXiv's cs.RO category (2015-early 2026), quantifying mentions of sustainability impacts and mapping them to UN SDGs through systematic content analysis.

Result: Sustainability mentions typically below 2%, explicit SDG references below 0.1%, and sustainability-motivated papers below 5%. While many robotics papers can be mapped to SDG-relevant domains, explicit sustainability motivation remains remarkably low.

Conclusion: Sustainability is not yet a standard part of robotics research framing despite the field's rapid advancement. The paper proposes concrete actions for researchers, conferences, and institutions to close awareness and motivation gaps for more intentional and responsible innovation.

Abstract: We present a large-scale survey of sustainability communication and motivation in robotics research. Our analysis covers nearly 50,000 open-access papers from arXiv's cs.RO category published between 2015 and early 2026. In this study, we quantify how often papers mention social, ecological, and sustainability impacts, and we analyse their alignment with the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
  The results reveal a persistent gap between the field's potential and its stated intent. While a large fraction of robotics papers can be mapped to SDG-relevant domains, explicit sustainability motivation remains remarkably low. Specifically, mentions of sustainability-related impacts are typically below 2%, explicit SDG references stay below 0.1%, and the proportion of sustainability-motivated papers remains below 5%. These trends suggest that while the field of robotics is advancing rapidly, sustainability is not yet a standard part of research framing.
  We conclude by proposing concrete actions for researchers, conferences, and institutions to close these awareness and motivation gaps, supporting a shift toward more intentional and responsible innovation.

</details>


### [154] [RAGE-XY: RADAR-Aided Longitudinal and Lateral Forces Estimation For Autonomous Race Cars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07939)
*Davide Malvezzi,Nicola Musiu,Eugenio Mascaro,Francesco Iacovacci,Marko Bertogna*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RAGE-XY extends RAGE framework with online RADAR calibration and tricycle vehicle model for improved real-time estimation of vehicle dynamics using standard sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve real-time vehicle dynamics estimation by addressing sensor misalignment issues and expanding beyond lateral dynamics to include longitudinal forces, using only standard onboard sensors.

Method: Extended RAGE framework with online RADAR calibration module to handle sensor misalignment, and upgraded vehicle model from single-track to tricycle model to estimate rear longitudinal tire forces in addition to lateral dynamics.

Result: Improved accuracy and robustness in estimating both lateral and longitudinal vehicle dynamics, validated through high-fidelity simulations and real-world experiments on EAV-24 autonomous race car.

Conclusion: RAGE-XY successfully enhances vehicle dynamics estimation by addressing sensor calibration and expanding modeling capabilities, demonstrating practical improvements for autonomous racing applications.

Abstract: In this work, we present RAGE-XY, an extended version of RAGE, a real-time estimation framework that simultaneously infers vehicle velocity, tire slip angles, and the forces acting on the vehicle using only standard onboard sensors such as IMUs and RADARs. Compared to the original formulation, the proposed method incorporates an online RADAR calibration module, improving the accuracy of lateral velocity estimation in the presence of sensor misalignment. Furthermore, we extend the underlying vehicle model from a single-track approximation to a tricycle model, enabling the estimation of rear longitudinal tire forces in addition to lateral dynamics. We validate the proposed approach through both high-fidelity simulations and real-world experiments conducted on the EAV-24 autonomous race car, demonstrating improved accuracy and robustness in estimating both lateral and longitudinal vehicle dynamics.

</details>


### [155] [On-Policy Distillation of Language Models for Autonomous Vehicle Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07944)
*Amirhossein Afsharrad,Amirhesam Abedsoltan,Ahmadreza Moradipari,Sanjay Lall*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: On-policy generalized knowledge distillation (GKD) effectively transfers motion planning knowledge from large LLMs to smaller deployable models for autonomous driving, achieving near-teacher performance with 5× size reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs show strong potential for autonomous vehicle motion planning but face deployment challenges in resource-constrained onboard systems due to their large size and computational requirements.

Method: Two student training paradigms: (1) on-policy generalized knowledge distillation (GKD) trains student on self-generated outputs using dense token-level feedback from teacher LLM, and (2) dense-feedback reinforcement learning baseline using teacher's log-probabilities as per-token reward signals.

Result: GKD substantially outperforms the RL baseline and closely approaches teacher-level performance despite a 5× reduction in model size on the nuScenes benchmark.

Conclusion: On-policy distillation is a principled and effective approach for deploying LLM-based planners in autonomous driving systems, enabling knowledge transfer from large teacher models to smaller deployable students.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated strong potential for autonomous vehicle motion planning by reformulating trajectory prediction as a language generation problem. However, deploying capable LLMs in resource-constrained onboard systems remains a fundamental challenge. In this paper, we study how to effectively transfer motion planning knowledge from a large teacher LLM to a smaller, more deployable student model. We build on the GPT-Driver framework, which represents driving scenes as language prompts and generates waypoint trajectories with chain-of-thought reasoning, and investigate two student training paradigms: (i) on-policy generalized knowledge distillation (GKD), which trains the student on its own self-generated outputs using dense token-level feedback from the teacher, and (ii) a dense-feedback reinforcement learning (RL) baseline that uses the teacher's log-probabilities as per-token reward signals in a policy gradient framework. Experiments on the nuScenes benchmark show that GKD substantially outperforms the RL baseline and closely approaches teacher-level performance despite a 5$\times$ reduction in model size. These results highlight the practical value of on-policy distillation as a principled and effective approach to deploying LLM-based planners in autonomous driving systems.

</details>


### [156] [Incremental Residual Reinforcement Learning Toward Real-World Learning for Social Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07945)
*Haruto Nagahisa,Kohei Matsumoto,Yuki Tomita,Yuki Hyodo,Ryo Kurazume*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IRRL combines incremental learning (lightweight, no replay buffer) with residual RL (efficient training on policy residuals) for real-world social navigation, achieving comparable performance to buffer-based methods and enabling effective adaptation to unseen environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Social navigation for mobile robots faces challenges due to diverse pedestrian dynamics and social conventions across regions. Simulations can't cover all real-world scenarios, and real-world RL approaches struggle with limited computational resources on edge devices and learning efficiency.

Method: Incremental Residual RL (IRRL) integrates incremental learning (lightweight process without replay buffer or batch updates) with residual RL (trains only on residuals relative to a base policy) to enhance learning efficiency and reduce computational requirements.

Result: Simulation experiments showed IRRL achieved performance comparable to conventional replay buffer-based methods despite lacking a replay buffer, and outperformed existing incremental learning approaches. Real-world experiments confirmed IRRL enables robots to effectively adapt to previously unseen environments through real-world learning.

Conclusion: IRRL provides an effective solution for real-world social navigation by combining the computational efficiency of incremental learning with the learning efficiency of residual RL, enabling practical adaptation to diverse real-world environments without requiring extensive simulation coverage.

Abstract: As the demand for mobile robots continues to increase, social navigation has emerged as a critical task, driving active research into deep reinforcement learning (RL) approaches. However, because pedestrian dynamics and social conventions vary widely across different regions, simulations cannot easily encompass all possible real-world scenarios. Real-world RL, in which agents learn while operating directly in physical environments, presents a promising solution to this issue. Nevertheless, this approach faces significant challenges, particularly regarding constrained computational resources on edge devices and learning efficiency. In this study, we propose incremental residual RL (IRRL). This method integrates incremental learning, which is a lightweight process that operates without a replay buffer or batch updates, with residual RL, which enhances learning efficiency by training only on the residuals relative to a base policy. Through the simulation experiments, we demonstrated that, despite lacking a replay buffer, IRRL achieved performance comparable to those of conventional replay buffer-based methods and outperformed existing incremental learning approaches. Furthermore, the real-world experiments confirmed that IRRL can enable robots to effectively adapt to previously unseen environments through the real-world learning.

</details>


### [157] [HEX: Humanoid-Aligned Experts for Cross-Embodiment Whole-Body Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07993)
*Shuanghao Bai,Meng Li,Xinyuan Lv,Jiawei Wang,Xinhua Wang,Fei Liao,Chengkai Hou,Langzhe Gu,Wanqi Zhou,Kun Wu,Ziluo Ding,Zhiyuan Xu,Lei Sun,Shanghang Zhang,Zhengping Che,Jian Tang,Badong Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HEX is a state-centric VLA framework for coordinated whole-body control of bipedal humanoids, using humanoid-aligned state representation, Mixture-of-Experts proprioceptive predictor, lightweight history tokens, and residual-gated fusion for stable, high-DoF manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models treat robot body parts independently, making high-DoF humanoid control challenging and unstable. Humans achieve complex manipulation through coordinated whole-body control, which existing approaches lack.

Method: 1) Humanoid-aligned universal state representation for scalable learning across embodiments; 2) Mixture-of-Experts Unified Proprioceptive Predictor for whole-body coordination and temporal dynamics; 3) Lightweight history tokens to summarize past observations efficiently; 4) Residual-gated fusion with flow-matching action head to integrate visual-language cues with proprioceptive dynamics.

Result: HEX achieves state-of-the-art performance in real-world humanoid manipulation tasks, particularly excelling in fast-reaction and long-horizon scenarios, with high task success rates and strong generalization capabilities.

Conclusion: HEX demonstrates that coordinated whole-body control through state-centric design and efficient temporal modeling enables stable, high-performance manipulation on full-sized bipedal humanoids, addressing key limitations of current VLA approaches.

Abstract: Humans achieve complex manipulation through coordinated whole-body control, whereas most Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models treat robot body parts largely independently, making high-DoF humanoid control challenging and often unstable. We present HEX, a state-centric framework for coordinated manipulation on full-sized bipedal humanoid robots. HEX introduces a humanoid-aligned universal state representation for scalable learning across heterogeneous embodiments, and incorporates a Mixture-of-Experts Unified Proprioceptive Predictor to model whole-body coordination and temporal motion dynamics from large-scale multi-embodiment trajectory data. To efficiently capture temporal visual context, HEX uses lightweight history tokens to summarize past observations, avoiding repeated encoding of historical images during inference. It further employs a residual-gated fusion mechanism with a flow-matching action head to adaptively integrate visual-language cues with proprioceptive dynamics for action generation. Experiments on real-world humanoid manipulation tasks show that HEX achieves state-of-the-art performance in task success rate and generalization, particularly in fast-reaction and long-horizon scenarios.

</details>


### [158] [AgiPIX: Bridging Simulation and Reality in Indoor Aerial Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08009)
*Sasanka Kuruppu Arachchige,Juan Jose Garcia,Changda Tian,Lauri Suomela,Panos Trahanias,Adriana Tapus,Joni-Kristian Kämäräinen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Agipix is an open-source hardware/software platform for indoor drone autonomy featuring compact active sensing, containerized ROS 2 software, and photorealistic digital twin for seamless simulation-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
